Constructors enforce that objects are created in a valid, initialized state. Without a constructor, fields would have default values (null, 0) regardless of business rules — leading to invalid objects that fail later in unexpected ways.

3. Advantages

  • Guarantees objects are always initialized in a valid state
  • Constructor overloading provides multiple convenient creation paths
  • Private constructor enforces Singleton or Factory patterns
  • Static constructor runs class-level initialization exactly once

4. Small C# Example

public class BankAccount
{
    public string AccountNumber { get; }
    public string HolderName { get; }
    public decimal Balance { get; private set; }
    public DateTime CreatedAt { get; }

    // 1. Parameterized constructor (primary)
    public BankAccount(string accountNumber, string holderName, decimal initialBalance)
    {
        if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(accountNumber))
            throw new ArgumentException("Account number required");
        if (initialBalance < 0)
            throw new ArgumentException("Initial balance cannot be negative");

        AccountNumber = accountNumber;
        HolderName = holderName;
        Balance = initialBalance;
        CreatedAt = DateTime.UtcNow;
    }

    // 2. Constructor chaining: convenience overload calls primary via :this()
    public BankAccount(string accountNumber, string holderName)
        : this(accountNumber, holderName, 0m) { }

    // 3. Copy constructor (manual in C#)
    public BankAccount(BankAccount source)
        : this(source.AccountNumber + "-COPY", source.HolderName, source.Balance) { }

    // 4. Static constructor: runs once before any instance created or static member accessed
    private static readonly HashSet<string> _reservedNumbers;
    static BankAccount()
    {
        _reservedNumbers = new HashSet<string> { "0000000000", "9999999999" };
        Console.WriteLine("BankAccount type initialized"); // runs once
    }

    // 5. Private constructor: used for Singleton or Factory
    private BankAccount() { }
    public static BankAccount CreateEmpty()
        => new BankAccount { Balance = 0 }; // uses private constructor
}

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

The BankAccount constructor validates AccountNumber and initial balance — you cannot create an account with a null number or negative balance. The convenience overload creates a zero-balance account for initial setup. The static constructor loads reserved account number ranges from a file once at startup. The private constructor is used by a Factory method that applies business-specific account creation rules before handing back a valid instance.

6. Interview Explanation

Mention constructor chaining (:this()) to avoid duplicate initialization code. Mention static constructor gotcha: you cannot control when it runs, you cannot call it explicitly, and if it throws, the type becomes permanently unusable for the AppDomain lifetime. Mention that once you define any constructor, the compiler no longer generates the default parameterless constructor automatically.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

TypeWhen CalledUse Case
Defaultnew MyClass()Zero-arg creation
Parameterizednew MyClass(args)Enforce required state
StaticBefore first use of typeOne-time class initialization
PrivateVia Factory/SingletonControlled instantiation

8. Best Interview Answer

C# has five constructor types: default (no params, auto-generated if no other constructor is defined), parameterized (enforces required initial state), copy (manual in C#, copies another instance), static (runs once before first use of the type for class-level initialization), and private (prevents direct instantiation — used for Singleton and Factory patterns). Constructor chaining with :this() avoids duplicate initialization code — convenience overloads delegate to a primary constructor with all the validation logic. When you define any constructor, the compiler stops generating the default one — callers must use your constructors.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Constructors are like assembly instructions in a factory: default = basic assembly, parameterized = custom order, static = the factory setup that happens once before any product is made, private = only the factory manager can start the assembly line.

followUps.question: What is constructor chaining and why is it useful?
followUps.answer:
Constructor chaining calls one constructor from another in the same class using : this() syntax, or calls the parent class constructor using : base(). It avoids duplicate initialisation logic.

public class Order
{
    public int Id { get; }
    public string Status { get; }
    public DateTime CreatedAt { get; }

    // Primary constructor with all logic
    public Order(int id, string status, DateTime createdAt)
    {
        Id = id; Status = status; CreatedAt = createdAt;
    }

    // Convenience overloads chain to the primary
    public Order(int id) : this(id, "Pending", DateTime.UtcNow) { }
    public Order(int id, string status) : this(id, status, DateTime.UtcNow) { }
}

Using : base(args) calls the parent class constructor before the child's body runs, ensuring the parent is properly initialised first.

questions.tip: Static constructor gotcha: You can't control when a static constructor runs (CLR decides), you can't call it explicitly, and if it throws, the type becomes unusable for the lifetime of the AppDomain.

questions.question: What are the different uses of the 'using' keyword in C#?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

The using keyword in C# has four distinct meanings: importing namespaces (using directive), deterministic resource cleanup (using statement), scope-based disposal without braces (using declaration in C# 8+), and project-wide namespace imports (global using in C# 10+).

2. Why It Is Used

The resource management forms of using (statement and declaration) guarantee that Dispose() is called on IDisposable objects even if an exception occurs — equivalent to try/finally but much more readable.

3. Advantages

  • Guarantees resource cleanup even on exceptions — no finally boilerplate
  • Global using reduces repetitive imports across large codebases
  • Type aliases resolve naming conflicts between same-named types
  • C# 8 using declaration eliminates nesting — cleaner code

4. Small C# Example

// 1. Using directive: import a namespace
using System.Data.SqlClient;
using System.Text;
using static System.Math;          // static: import static members directly
using DecimalList = System.Collections.Generic.List<decimal>; // type alias

// 2. Using statement (C# 1+): scoped disposal with braces
public string GenerateBankStatement(string accountId)
{
    using (var conn = new SqlConnection(_connString))
    using (var cmd = conn.CreateCommand())
    {
        conn.Open();
        cmd.CommandText = "SELECT * FROM Transactions WHERE AccountId = @id";
        cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@id", accountId);
        using var reader = cmd.ExecuteReader();
        var sb = new StringBuilder();
        while (reader.Read())
            sb.AppendLine($"{reader["Date"]}: {reader["Amount"]:C}");
        return sb.ToString();
    } // conn and cmd disposed here
}

// 3. Using declaration (C# 8+): disposed at end of enclosing scope
public async Task ProcessTransactionFileAsync(string path)
{
    using var file = File.OpenRead(path);    // no braces needed
    using var reader = new StreamReader(file);
    string? line;
    while ((line = await reader.ReadLineAsync()) != null)
    {
        await ProcessLineAsync(line);
    }
} // file and reader disposed here when method ends

// 4. Global using (C# 10+): applies to entire project
// In GlobalUsings.cs:
// global using System.Text.Json;
// global using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
// global using PaymentPlatform.Models;

// 5. Async disposal: IAsyncDisposable
public async Task ProcessAsync()
{
    await using var dbContext = new PaymentDbContext();
    // dbContext.DisposeAsync() called at end
}

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

In a payment data export job: using declarations for SqlConnection, SqlCommand, and StreamWriter guarantee all connections and files are closed even if an exception occurs mid-export. Without using, a failed export could leave database connections open and exhaust the connection pool. Global using eliminates the need to write using System.Text.Json and using PaymentPlatform.Models in every file across the 50+ controller and service files.

6. Interview Explanation

Show you know all four uses — most candidates only know the first two. Mention that using statement is compiled to try/finally under the hood. Mention IAsyncDisposable and await using for async cleanup. Note that C# 8 using declaration is cleaner for methods that open one resource and use it for the entire method scope.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FormC# VersionPurpose
using directiveAllImport namespace
using statementAllScoped disposal (braces)
using declarationC# 8+Scoped disposal (no braces)
global usingC# 10+Project-wide namespace import

8. Best Interview Answer

The using keyword has four uses in C#. First, namespace import: using System.Collections.Generic. Second, using statement: using (var conn = new SqlConnection(...)) { } — compiles to try/finally, guarantees Dispose() on scope exit. Third, using declaration (C# 8+): using var conn = new SqlConnection(...) — no braces, disposed at end of enclosing scope. Fourth, global using (C# 10+): global using MyNamespace — imports the namespace for every file in the project. I use C# 8 using declarations in all services — they are cleaner than the brace-based statement for single-resource scopes.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

using for cleanup = "I'm done with this, clean it up automatically when I leave." The compiler writes the try/finally for you so you don't forget.

followUps.question: What happens if Dispose() throws an exception inside a using block?
followUps.answer: If the body of the using block throws an exception and Dispose() also throws, the original exception is lost — only the Dispose exception propagates. This is a known C# design trade-off. Best practice: write Dispose() so it never throws (catch any internal exceptions and log them instead). This is one reason the IDisposable pattern typically has a try/catch inside Dispose(bool disposing).

questions.tip: Rule of thumb: Any class that holds unmanaged resources (file handles, DB connections, network sockets) should implement IDisposable, and callers should always wrap it in using.

questions.question: What is early binding and late binding in C#? Give real examples.
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Early binding (static binding) means the compiler resolves which method to call at compile time — the call target is fixed in the IL. Late binding (dynamic binding) means the method to call is determined at runtime — used with virtual methods (vtable dispatch), the dynamic keyword, and reflection.

2. Why It Is Used

Early binding is the default and is fastest — no runtime overhead. Late binding is used when the concrete type is not known at compile time: polymorphism (virtual dispatch), plugin loading (reflection), COM interop (dynamic), or LINQ expression trees (expression-based late binding for SQL translation).

3. Advantages

  • Early binding: maximum performance, full IntelliSense, compile-time errors
  • Late binding via virtual: enables polymorphism — the core OOP benefit
  • Late binding via reflection: enables plugin architectures and generic frameworks

4. Small C# Example

// Early binding: method resolved at compile time
var calculator = new FeeCalculator();
decimal fee = calculator.Calculate(5000m);  // compiler knows exactly which Calculate to call

// Late binding via virtual dispatch (polymorphism)
public abstract class PaymentGateway
{
    public abstract Task<bool> ChargeAsync(decimal amount, string token);
}

public class RazorpayGateway : PaymentGateway
{
    public override async Task<bool> ChargeAsync(decimal amount, string token)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"Razorpay: charging {amount}");
        return true;
    }
}

public class PayUGateway : PaymentGateway
{
    public override async Task<bool> ChargeAsync(decimal amount, string token)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"PayU: charging {amount}");
        return true;
    }
}

// Late binding at runtime — which ChargeAsync runs?
PaymentGateway gateway = GetGatewayFromConfig(); // returns Razorpay or PayU at runtime
await gateway.ChargeAsync(5000m, "tok_123");     // vtable dispatch decides at runtime

// Late binding via dynamic keyword
dynamic response = await httpClient.GetFromJsonAsync<dynamic>("/api/rates");
Console.WriteLine(response.usdToInr); // resolved at runtime via DLR

// Late binding via Reflection
Type serviceType = Type.GetType("PaymentPlatform.Services.RazorpayGateway")!;
var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(serviceType);
var method = serviceType.GetMethod("ChargeAsync");
var task = (Task<bool>)method!.Invoke(instance, new object[] { 5000m, "tok_123" })!;
bool charged = await task;

public static PaymentGateway GetGatewayFromConfig()
    => new RazorpayGateway(); // example

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

The payment routing engine holds a PaymentGateway reference and calls ChargeAsync() — late binding via vtable selects RazorpayGateway or PayUGateway at runtime based on what was injected. This is the core of the strategy pattern. Reflection-based late binding is used in the payment plugin system: partner bank plugins are loaded as DLLs at startup, their types are discovered via reflection, and IPaymentGateway implementations are registered in DI dynamically — no compile-time reference needed.

6. Interview Explanation

Connect late binding to polymorphism — virtual/override dispatch is the most common form of late binding in C# and is the mechanism behind the strategy, template method, and factory patterns. Distinguish between the three types: virtual dispatch (fast, type-safe), dynamic (DLR, slower), and reflection (slowest, most flexible).

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

Binding TypeWhen ResolvedPerformanceType Safety
Early (static)Compile timeFastestFull
Late via virtualRuntime (vtable)Near nativeFull
Late via dynamicRuntime (DLR)SlowerNone at compile time
Late via reflectionRuntimeSlowestRuntime only

8. Best Interview Answer

Early binding means the compiler decides which method to call at compile time — it is the default for all regular method calls and produces the most efficient IL. Late binding defers that decision to runtime. The most common form is virtual dispatch: calling ChargeAsync() on a PaymentGateway reference dispatches to the actual implementation (Razorpay or PayU) at runtime via the vtable — this is polymorphism. The dynamic keyword uses the DLR for runtime dispatch with no compile-time type checking. Reflection uses GetMethod().Invoke() — most flexible but slowest. In fintech, virtual dispatch powers the payment strategy pattern; reflection powers plugin discovery at startup.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Early binding = GPS with a fixed route calculated before you leave. Late binding = navigation that decides turn by turn as you drive. Virtual dispatch is a fast GPS; reflection is asking for directions at every intersection.

followUps.question: How does virtual method dispatch (vtable) work?
followUps.answer: Every class with virtual methods has a vtable (virtual dispatch table) — an array of function pointers, one per virtual method. When an object is created, its vtable pointer is set to the vtable of its actual type. When you call animal.Speak() on a Dog instance through an Animal reference, the CLR looks up Speak in Dog's vtable and calls it — regardless of what the reference variable's declared type is. This is how polymorphism works at the machine level. The lookup adds one level of indirection compared to a direct (early-bound) call, but in practice the overhead is negligible.

questions.tip: Interview link: Late binding via virtual dispatch = polymorphism. Late binding via dynamic/reflection = runtime flexibility at the cost of type safety and performance.

questions.question: Can an abstract class have a parameterized constructor? How do you call it from a derived class?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Yes, an abstract class can have a parameterized constructor. Since abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly, their constructors are always called through derived class constructors using the : base(...) syntax. The derived class must explicitly call the base constructor to initialize base class fields.

2. Why It Is Used

Abstract class constructors initialize shared state that all derived classes need. Rather than duplicating initialization in every derived class, the base constructor centralizes it. The : base() call enforces that derived classes provide the required initialization data.

3. Advantages

  • Centralizes common initialization in one place
  • Compiler enforces that derived classes supply required arguments via :base()
  • Base class invariants are established before derived class constructor runs

4. Small C# Example

public abstract class BasePaymentProcessor
{
    public string GatewayName { get; }
    protected readonly ILogger _logger;
    protected readonly decimal _maxTransactionLimit;

    // Abstract class constructor: always protected — no point being public
    protected BasePaymentProcessor(string gatewayName, decimal maxLimit, ILogger logger)
    {
        if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(gatewayName))
            throw new ArgumentException("Gateway name required");
        GatewayName = gatewayName;
        _maxTransactionLimit = maxLimit;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    // Abstract method: derived classes must implement
    public abstract Task<PaymentResult> ChargeAsync(decimal amount, string token);

    // Shared validation using base class fields
    protected void ValidateAmount(decimal amount)
    {
        if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");
        if (amount > _maxTransactionLimit)
            throw new InvalidOperationException(
                $"{GatewayName}: amount {amount} exceeds limit {_maxTransactionLimit}");
    }
}

public class RazorpayProcessor : BasePaymentProcessor
{
    private readonly string _apiKey;

    // Must call base constructor with required args
    public RazorpayProcessor(string apiKey, ILogger<RazorpayProcessor> logger)
        : base("Razorpay", 500000m, logger)  // base(...) called first
    {
        _apiKey = apiKey;
    }

    public override async Task<PaymentResult> ChargeAsync(decimal amount, string token)
    {
        ValidateAmount(amount); // uses base class method + base class _maxTransactionLimit
        _logger.LogInformation("{Gateway}: charging {Amount}", GatewayName, amount);
        return new PaymentResult { Success = true };
    }
}

public record PaymentResult(bool Success = false, string? Error = null);

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

BasePaymentProcessor holds the gateway name, logger, and transaction limit — common to all gateways. RazorpayProcessor and PayUProcessor both call : base("Razorpay", 500000m, logger) to initialize the shared state. ValidateAmount() in the base class uses _maxTransactionLimit — a field that only the base constructor can set because it is private. This ensures every gateway enforces its own limit consistently without copy-pasting the validation logic.

6. Interview Explanation

Key points: abstract classes CAN have constructors (even parameterized ones). They CANNOT be called directly via new. The : base() syntax in the derived constructor calls the base constructor BEFORE the derived constructor body runs. If the abstract class has no default constructor, all derived classes MUST provide : base(...) or the code won't compile.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

Constructor OrderExecution
new RazorpayProcessor(...) called1. :base() resolves and runs BasePaymentProcessor constructor
Base constructor completes2. RazorpayProcessor constructor body runs

8. Best Interview Answer

Yes, abstract classes can have parameterized constructors. They are typically marked protected since the class cannot be instantiated directly. Derived classes call the base constructor using : base(args) in their constructor declaration — this runs the base constructor before the derived constructor body. If the abstract class has only a parameterized constructor and no default, all derived classes must provide the base() call explicitly or the code will not compile. This pattern is how base classes enforce shared initialization across all implementations.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Abstract class constructor = the foundation of a building. You can't live in the foundation (can't instantiate), but every floor (derived class) must be built on top of it first (:base() runs first).

followUps.question: What is the order of constructor execution in an inheritance chain?
followUps.answer:
Constructors run from the top of the hierarchy down — the base class constructor always completes before the derived class constructor body runs. For a three-level chain A → B → C:

  1. C's constructor is called
  2. Before C's body runs, : base() invokes B's constructor
  3. Before B's body runs, : base() invokes A's constructor
  4. A's body executes first
  5. Then B's body
  6. Finally C's body

This guarantees that base class invariants are established before the derived class can use them.

questions.tip: Common mistake: Forgetting that if you add a parameterized constructor to an abstract base class without keeping a default constructor, all existing derived classes will fail to compile until they add : base(...).

questions.question: Can a class implement two interfaces that have the same method name? How do you handle conflicts?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Yes, a class can implement two interfaces with the same method name. If the signatures match and the behavior should be the same, one method body satisfies both. If the behavior should differ per interface, use explicit interface implementation — prefix the method with the interface name. Explicitly implemented methods are only accessible through the interface reference.

2. Why It Is Used

Explicit implementation resolves naming conflicts between interfaces, hides interface methods from the class's public API, and enables different behavior per interface for the same method name.

3. Advantages

  • Allows implementing conflicting interfaces without compile errors
  • Explicit implementation keeps the class's public API clean
  • Different behavior per interface without exposing duplicate public methods

4. Small C# Example

// Case 1: Same signature, same behavior — one method satisfies both
interface IPaymentLogger  { void Log(string message); }
interface IAuditLogger    { void Log(string message); }

public class UnifiedLogger : IPaymentLogger, IAuditLogger
{
    // One method, satisfies both interfaces
    public void Log(string message)
        => Console.WriteLine($"[LOG] {message}");
}

var logger = new UnifiedLogger();
logger.Log("Test");              // public method
((IPaymentLogger)logger).Log("Test"); // same
((IAuditLogger)logger).Log("Test");   // same

// Case 2: Same signature, DIFFERENT behavior — explicit implementation
interface IUpiProcessor  { Task<bool> ProcessAsync(decimal amount); }
interface INeftProcessor { Task<bool> ProcessAsync(decimal amount); }

public class HybridPaymentProcessor : IUpiProcessor, INeftProcessor
{
    // Explicit: only accessible via interface reference
    async Task<bool> IUpiProcessor.ProcessAsync(decimal amount)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"UPI: instant transfer of {amount}");
        return amount <= 100000m;
    }

    async Task<bool> INeftProcessor.ProcessAsync(decimal amount)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"NEFT: batch transfer of {amount}");
        return amount >= 1m;
    }
}

HybridPaymentProcessor p = new();
// p.ProcessAsync(1000m);  // COMPILE ERROR — not directly accessible

((IUpiProcessor)p).ProcessAsync(1000m);  // UPI logic
((INeftProcessor)p).ProcessAsync(1000m); // NEFT logic

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

HybridPaymentProcessor implements both IUpiProcessor and INeftProcessor. UPI has instant transfer with a Rs.1 lakh limit; NEFT is batch with no upper limit. Both interfaces have ProcessAsync(decimal) but the logic is different — explicit implementation keeps them separate. The payment router casts to the correct interface based on the payment rail selected, ensuring the right processing logic runs.

6. Interview Explanation

Key rule for explicit implementation: the method is only accessible when the object is accessed through the interface reference. On the concrete class type, it is invisible. This is intentional — it keeps the class API clean when the interface methods are not part of its natural public surface. Also mention: you cannot mark explicitly implemented methods as public or virtual.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

ImplementationAccessible on class typeAccessible via interface
Implicit (public)YesYes
Explicit (InterfaceName.Method)NoYes

8. Best Interview Answer

Yes. If both interfaces have the same method signature and the behavior should be the same, one public method satisfies both — the compiler routes both interface calls to it. If the behavior should differ, use explicit interface implementation: void IUpiProcessor.ProcessAsync() and void INeftProcessor.ProcessAsync() — each prefixed with the interface name. Explicitly implemented methods are hidden on the concrete class and only accessible via the interface reference. I use explicit implementation to resolve naming conflicts and to hide interface members that are not part of the class's natural public API.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Explicit interface implementation = a disguise. When you look at the class directly, the method is hidden. Only when you look through the interface lens do you see it.

followUps.question: Can an interface have a default implementation (default interface methods)?
followUps.answer:
Yes, since C# 8, interfaces can have default method implementations. This allows adding new methods to existing interfaces without breaking all implementing classes.

interface IShape
{
    double Area();
    // Default implementation — implementing classes can override or use this
    string Describe() => $"This shape has area {Area():F2}";
}

class Circle : IShape
{
    public double Radius { get; set; }
    public double Area() => Math.PI * Radius * Radius;
    // No need to implement Describe() — uses the default
}

IShape s = new Circle { Radius = 3 };
Console.WriteLine(s.Describe()); // works via default implementation

Default interface methods are only accessible through the interface reference, not the concrete type.

questions.tip: Explicit implementation rule: The method is only accessible through the interface reference. This is intentional — it keeps the class's public API clean while still fulfilling the interface contract.

questions.question: What is the difference between Array and ArrayList in C#? When do you use List<T>?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Array is a fixed-size, strongly-typed collection — size is set at creation and cannot change. ArrayList is a legacy dynamic-size collection that stores object — not type-safe, requires casting, and boxes value types. List<T> is the modern generic dynamic collection — type-safe, no boxing, no casting. Use List<T> in all new code.

2. Why It Is Used

Array is used when size is known and fixed. List<T> replaced ArrayList in .NET 2.0 when generics were introduced — it is the correct choice for all dynamic collections in modern C#.

3. Advantages

  • Array: fixed size, zero-copy access, works with Span<T>, fastest random access
  • List<T>: dynamic sizing, type-safe, no boxing, LINQ-friendly
  • ArrayList: nothing in modern code — legacy only

4. Small C# Example

// Array: fixed size, typed
string[] paymentMethods = new string[4] { "UPI", "NEFT", "IMPS", "Card" };
decimal[] dailyTotals = new decimal[31]; // one entry per day of month, fixed

// paymentMethods[4] = "RTGS"; // IndexOutOfRangeException — can't grow

// ArrayList: dynamic, but stores object — avoid in new code
var legacyCollection = new System.Collections.ArrayList();
legacyCollection.Add(5000m);      // decimal BOXED to object!
legacyCollection.Add("UPI-REF");  // no type safety — mixed types allowed
legacyCollection.Add(42);         // int BOXED
decimal amount = (decimal)legacyCollection[0]; // cast required, risky

// List<T>: dynamic, typed — use this always
var transactions = new List<Transaction>();
transactions.Add(new Transaction { Id = 1, Amount = 5000m });
transactions.Add(new Transaction { Id = 2, Amount = 3000m });
// transactions.Add("not a transaction"); // COMPILE ERROR — type safety
Transaction t = transactions[0]; // no cast needed

// When Array IS better: known fixed size, Span operations
byte[] headerBytes = new byte[4];
ReadOnlySpan<byte> span = headerBytes.AsSpan(); // zero-copy slice

// Pre-allocate List when size is approximately known
var results = new List<Transaction>(capacity: 1000); // avoid resizing

public class Transaction
{
    public int Id { get; init; }
    public decimal Amount { get; init; }
}

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

Transaction processing: List<Transaction> builds up the collection as transactions arrive — dynamic, type-safe, LINQ-friendly for filtering and sorting. The 4-byte transaction header is a byte[] array — fixed size, works directly with Span<byte> for zero-copy parsing. An old bank middleware integration might use ArrayList internally — when reading its data, cast immediately to List<T> and never pass ArrayList further into modern code.

6. Interview Explanation

Emphasize: ArrayList is legacy — any code using ArrayList in a .NET 2.0+ project is a code smell. The specific problem: ArrayList stores object, so every decimal/int/bool stored in it gets boxed. In a payment system with millions of transactions, that is millions of unnecessary heap allocations. List<T> avoids all of them.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FeatureArrayArrayListList<T>
SizeFixedDynamicDynamic
Type safetyYesNoYes
BoxingNoYesNo
Use in new codeWhen size is fixedNeverDefault choice

8. Best Interview Answer

Array is fixed-size and strongly typed — use it when the size is known upfront and doesn't change. ArrayList is a legacy type from .NET 1.x — it stores object, requires casting, and boxes value types. It should never appear in modern C# code. List<T> is the correct replacement: dynamic sizing, type-safe, no boxing, full LINQ support. The only reason to use Array over List<T> in new code is when size is fixed and you need to work with Span<T> for zero-copy buffer operations.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Array = a parking lot with fixed spaces. ArrayList = a parking lot that accepts cars, trucks, and bicycles mixed (object) — chaos. List<T> = a parking lot that only accepts cars (type-safe) and can add more spaces as needed.

followUps.question: When is it better to use an array over List<T>?
followUps.answer:
Prefer arrays when:

  • The size is fixed and known (e.g., RGB values — always 3 elements).
  • You need maximum performance — arrays avoid the overhead of List's internal size checks.
  • Working with interop / unsafe code that requires contiguous memory.
  • Multidimensional data — int[,] or int[][] are natural; List of Lists is clunky.
  • Spans and Memory<T> — Span<T> works directly over arrays for zero-copy slicing.

In most application-level code, List<T> is simpler and the performance difference is negligible. Use arrays in hot paths, parsing, serialisation, or when returning from a method where the caller shouldn't modify the size.

questions.tip: Interview shortcut: ArrayList = legacy, untyped, causes boxing. List<T> = modern, typed, no boxing. Use List<T> always unless you need a fixed-size typed array.

questions.question: What is the difference between virtual/override and the new keyword for method hiding in C#?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

virtual + override enables true runtime polymorphism — calling through a base reference dispatches to the derived implementation via the vtable. The new keyword hides the base method — when called through a base reference, the BASE method runs, not the derived one. This is a fundamental difference that many developers get wrong.

2. Why It Is Used

virtual/override is used when you want the correct derived behavior to execute regardless of what reference type holds the object. new is used (rarely) when you intentionally want to hide a base member without participating in virtual dispatch — a deliberate break in the polymorphic chain.

3. Advantages

  • virtual/override: true polymorphism — the object's actual type determines behavior
  • new: occasionally useful when a derived class intentionally provides a different unrelated method
  • Understanding the difference prevents subtle bugs where expected polymorphism doesn't work

4. Small C# Example

// virtual + override: TRUE polymorphism
public class PaymentProcessor
{
    public virtual string GetGatewayName() => "Base";
}

public class RazorpayProcessor : PaymentProcessor
{
    public override string GetGatewayName() => "Razorpay";
}

PaymentProcessor p1 = new RazorpayProcessor();
Console.WriteLine(p1.GetGatewayName()); // "Razorpay" -- override, vtable dispatch

// new keyword: METHOD HIDING — breaks polymorphic chain
public class UpiProcessor : PaymentProcessor
{
    public new string GetGatewayName() => "UPI"; // HIDES base method
}

UpiProcessor u1 = new UpiProcessor();
Console.WriteLine(u1.GetGatewayName()); // "UPI" -- direct call, derived type

PaymentProcessor u2 = new UpiProcessor();
Console.WriteLine(u2.GetGatewayName()); // "Base" -- SURPRISE! base method called!
// Because GetGatewayName is NOT in UpiProcessor's vtable override — it's hidden

// The critical interview question:
PaymentProcessor[] gateways = {
    new RazorpayProcessor(), // override
    new UpiProcessor()       // new (hiding)
};
foreach (var g in gateways)
    Console.WriteLine(g.GetGatewayName());
// Output:
// "Razorpay" -- override dispatches to derived
// "Base"     -- new hides, base method runs through base reference

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A payment routing engine holds a List<PaymentProcessor> and calls GetGatewayName() on each. With override, polymorphism works correctly — each processor returns its own name. If UpiProcessor uses new instead of override, calling through the base reference returns "Base" — the routing engine never sees "UPI". This type of bug is invisible at compile time and only surfaces in integration tests when the wrong gateway is selected.

6. Interview Explanation

The classic interview test: what does Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak() print? If Speak is override → Dog's version. If Speak is new → Animal's version. Always emphasize: new breaks polymorphism. Use it only when you intentionally want to sever the virtual dispatch chain for a specific member.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

KeywordAccessed via derived refAccessed via base ref
overrideDerived methodDerived method (vtable)
newDerived methodBASE method (hiding!)

8. Best Interview Answer

virtual marks a base class method as overridable. override in the derived class replaces it — virtual dispatch ensures that even when the object is accessed through a base reference, the derived implementation runs. This is runtime polymorphism. The new keyword hides the base method — when accessed through a base reference, the base method runs. This is method hiding, not polymorphism. Rule: always use override for polymorphism. Use new only when intentionally breaking the virtual dispatch chain for a specific reason, and document why.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

override = "I am the new version of what was there before" (vtable updated, polymorphism works). new = "I am a completely separate thing that happens to have the same name" (no vtable entry, base reference ignores you).

followUps.question: What happens if you use 'new' without the keyword explicitly?
followUps.answer: If you define a method in a derived class with the same signature as a base class method, without marking it override or new, C# will compile it but issue a compiler warning: 'Dog.Speak()' hides inherited member 'Animal.Speak()'. Use the new keyword if hiding was intended. The behaviour is the same as new — the base method is hidden. The compiler warning is C#'s way of saying "this looks unintentional". Adding new explicitly silences the warning and documents the intent.

questions.tip: Key interview question: 'Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak(); what prints?' If Speak is override → Dog's version. If it's new → Animal's version. This tests whether you understand runtime dispatch vs compile-time resolution.

questions.question: What are extension methods in C#? How do you create one and when do you use them?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Extension methods add new methods to existing types without modifying the original type's source code — even sealed classes and third-party types. They are static methods in a static class with the first parameter prefixed by this. After importing the namespace, they appear as instance methods on the target type.

2. Why It Is Used

Extension methods enable adding functionality to types you don't own or cannot modify (sealed string, IEnumerable<T>, third-party DTOs). LINQ is built entirely on extension methods.

3. Advantages

  • Adds methods to any type including sealed classes and interfaces
  • Discovered by IntelliSense like instance methods
  • LINQ (Where, Select, OrderBy) are all extension methods on IEnumerable<T>
  • Can be called on null without NullReferenceException (unlike instance methods)

4. Small C# Example

// Extension methods must be in a static class, method must be static
public static class PaymentExtensions
{
    // Extends decimal with payment-specific formatting
    public static string ToRupees(this decimal amount)
        => $"\u20b9{amount:N2}";

    // Extends decimal with GST calculation
    public static decimal WithGst(this decimal amount, decimal gstRate = 0.18m)
        => Math.Round(amount * (1 + gstRate), 2);

    // Extends string with UPI validation
    public static bool IsValidUpiId(this string? upiId)
        => !string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(upiId) && upiId.Contains('@');

    // Extends IEnumerable<Transaction>
    public static decimal TotalAmount(this IEnumerable<Transaction> transactions)
        => transactions.Sum(t => t.Amount);

    public static IEnumerable<Transaction> AboveThreshold(
        this IEnumerable<Transaction> transactions, decimal threshold)
        => transactions.Where(t => t.Amount > threshold);
}

// Usage: called as if instance methods
decimal fee = 1000m.WithGst();                 // Rs.1180.00
string display = fee.ToRupees();               // "Rs.1,180.00"
bool valid = "darshan@okicici".IsValidUpiId(); // true
bool nullCheck = ((string?)null).IsValidUpiId(); // false, no NullRefException

var largeTransactions = transactions
    .AboveThreshold(50000m)   // custom extension
    .OrderByDescending(t => t.Amount) // LINQ extension
    .ToList();

decimal total = transactions.TotalAmount(); // custom extension

public record Transaction(int Id, decimal Amount);

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

decimal.WithGst() and decimal.ToRupees() are used across the entire payment platform — in controllers formatting API responses, in services calculating fees, in view models displaying amounts. Because these are extension methods on decimal, they appear in IntelliSense everywhere the type is used. The LINQ chain combining built-in extensions (OrderBy, Where) with custom ones (AboveThreshold, TotalAmount) reads like a natural language query.

6. Interview Explanation

LINQ connection is critical: every LINQ method is an extension method on IEnumerable<T> defined in System.Linq.Enumerable. When you write list.Where(x => x.Active), the compiler translates it to Enumerable.Where(list, x => x.Active). Mention: instance methods take priority over extension methods if both have the same signature. Extension methods cannot access private members of the target type.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FeatureInstance MethodExtension Method
Modifies typeYesNo
Access private membersYesNo
Works on sealed typesN/AYes
Called on nullNullReferenceExceptionWorks (if method handles null)

8. Best Interview Answer

Extension methods add methods to existing types without modifying them. They are static methods in a static class with the first parameter prefixed by this — the this parameter is the type being extended. After importing the namespace, they appear as instance methods in IntelliSense. LINQ is the most prominent example — Where, Select, OrderBy are all extension methods on IEnumerable<T>. I use extension methods to add domain-specific helpers to built-in types: decimal.WithGst(), string.IsValidUpiId(), IEnumerable<Transaction>.TotalAmount(). Limitations: cannot access private members, cannot override existing instance methods.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Extension method = sticking a Post-it note with new instructions on something you don't own. The original stays unchanged; you just gave it a new capability by adding your note.

followUps.question: Can extension methods be called on null references?
followUps.answer:
Yes — and this is one of their advantages. Because extension methods are really static method calls, the object can be null without throwing a NullReferenceException (as long as your extension method handles null):

public static bool IsNullOrEmpty(this string s) 
    => string.IsNullOrEmpty(s); // handles null safely

string s = null;
bool result = s.IsNullOrEmpty(); // fine — no NullReferenceException

This is why many utility extension methods do a null check on this at the start. Instance methods would throw if called on null.

questions.tip: LINQ connection: Every LINQ method (Where, Select, GroupBy) is an extension method on IEnumerable<T>. When you write list.Where(x => x > 0), the compiler translates it to Enumerable.Where(list, x => x > 0).

questions.question: What is CTS (Common Type System) and CLS (Common Language Specification) in .NET?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

CTS (Common Type System) defines all data types and programming constructs that the CLR supports and the rules for how they interact. CLS (Common Language Specification) is the subset of CTS that every .NET language must support to ensure cross-language interoperability. CLS compliance means your library works from C#, VB.NET, and F# without issues.

2. Why It Is Used

CTS enables C# int and VB.NET Integer to be the same type (System.Int32) — they interoperate seamlessly. CLS ensures that a library you publish can be consumed from any .NET language, not just C#.

3. Advantages

  • CTS ensures type consistency across all .NET languages
  • CLS compliance makes public libraries consumable from any .NET language
  • [CLSCompliant(true)] attribute makes the compiler warn on violations

4. Small C# Example

// CTS: C# int and VB.NET Integer are the same CTS type
int x = 42;                     // System.Int32
System.Int32 y = 42;            // identical
Console.WriteLine(x.GetType().FullName); // System.Int32

// CLS-compliant public API: use types all .NET languages support
[assembly: CLSCompliant(true)] // compiler will warn on violations

namespace PaymentPlatform.PublicApi
{
    public class TransactionService
    {
        // COMPLIANT: int, long, string, decimal are in CLS
        public decimal GetBalance(long accountId) => 50000m;

        // NOT CLS-compliant: uint, ulong, sbyte, ushort are NOT in CLS
        // VB.NET has no unsigned integer equivalent
        // public uint GetTransactionCount() => 42; // Warning: not CLS-compliant

        // FIX: use long instead of ulong
        public long GetTransactionCount() => 42L;
    }
}

// CTS value type categories
int number = 42;                // value type
string text = "hello";          // reference type (special)
DateTime date = DateTime.UtcNow; // value type (struct)
object obj = new object();      // reference type

// All .NET languages compile to IL using the same CTS types
// C# List<int> and F# list<int> are interoperable because both use System.Int32

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A payment SDK published as a NuGet package should be marked [CLSCompliant(true)]. The compiler then warns if any public method uses uint (transaction count), ulong (account balance in paisa — switch to long), or other non-CLS types. This ensures banks using VB.NET legacy systems or F# data processing pipelines can consume the SDK without type conversion issues.

6. Interview Explanation

Most developers never explicitly work with CTS or CLS — the CLR handles it transparently. It matters when writing cross-language libraries. The interview-ready summary: CTS = the full type system the runtime supports; CLS = the safe subset all languages understand; [CLSCompliant(true)] = tell the compiler to warn me if I break it in public APIs.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

ConceptScopePurpose
CTSFull CLR type systemDefines all types and rules
CLSSubset of CTSCross-language compatibility minimum

8. Best Interview Answer

CTS is the type system defined by the CLR — it specifies all types (value types: int, struct, enum; reference types: class, interface, delegate) and the rules for type safety and inheritance. CTS is why C# int and VB.NET Integer are the same type at runtime — both are System.Int32. CLS is a subset of CTS that represents the minimum feature set every .NET language must support for cross-language interoperability. CLS excludes types like uint, ulong, sbyte because not all .NET languages have equivalents. Mark a public library [CLSCompliant(true)] to get compiler warnings when public members use non-CLS types.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

CTS = the full menu at a restaurant (all food available). CLS = the items every restaurant in the chain must serve (the common minimum). If you only serve from the CLS menu, any customer from any .NET language can eat.

followUps.question: What is the difference between CTS value types and reference types at memory level?
followUps.answer: Value types (int, bool, struct, enum) are allocated on the stack — they live and die with the method call. When you assign a value type, you get a full copy — modifying the copy doesn't affect the original. Reference types (class, string, array, delegate) are allocated on the heap — the variable holds a reference (pointer) to the heap memory. When you assign a reference type, both variables point to the same object. This is why passing a class instance to a method lets the method modify the object, but passing an int doesn't. Stack is fast and self-managing; heap requires garbage collection. Understanding this distinction explains a lot of subtle C# bugs around mutation and unexpected side effects.

questions.tip: Interview shortcut: CTS = the full type system the runtime defines. CLS = the safe subset every language understands. CLSCompliant attribute = 'please warn me if I break CLS rules in my public API'.

questions.question: What is Reflection in .NET? When have you actually used it?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Reflection is the ability to inspect and interact with types, methods, properties, and attributes at runtime — without knowing them at compile time. You can discover type metadata, create instances dynamically, invoke methods, and read custom attributes, all at runtime.

2. Why It Is Used

Reflection enables generic frameworks, plugin systems, ORMs, serializers, and dependency injection containers — all of which must work with types they don't know about at compile time. It is the foundation of .NET DI, AutoMapper, EF Core, and JSON serializers.

3. Advantages

  • Enables plugin/extensibility architectures without compile-time references
  • Custom attribute processing for cross-cutting concerns (logging, validation, audit)
  • Generic frameworks (ORMs, DI containers, mappers) use reflection internally

4. Small C# Example

// Inspect type metadata
Type type = typeof(BankTransaction);
Console.WriteLine(type.Name);     // BankTransaction
Console.WriteLine(type.IsClass);  // true

// List all properties
foreach (var prop in type.GetProperties())
    Console.WriteLine($"{prop.Name}: {prop.PropertyType.Name}");

// Read property values at runtime
var txn = new BankTransaction { Id = 1, Amount = 5000m, UpiId = "test@upi" };
foreach (var prop in txn.GetType().GetProperties())
    Console.WriteLine($"{prop.Name} = {prop.GetValue(txn)}");

// Custom attribute for sensitive data masking in logs
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Property)]
public class SensitiveAttribute : Attribute { }

public class PaymentRequest
{
    public string TransactionId { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    [Sensitive] public string AccountNumber { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    [Sensitive] public string Cvv { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public decimal Amount { get; set; }
}

// Use reflection to find sensitive properties for log masking
public static string MaskSensitiveFields<T>(T obj)
{
    var sb = new System.Text.StringBuilder();
    foreach (var prop in typeof(T).GetProperties())
    {
        bool isSensitive = prop.IsDefined(typeof(SensitiveAttribute), false);
        string value = isSensitive ? "***MASKED***" : prop.GetValue(obj)?.ToString() ?? "";
        sb.AppendLine($"{prop.Name}: {value}");
    }
    return sb.ToString();
}

// Create instance dynamically (plugin system)
Type? serviceType = Type.GetType("PaymentPlatform.Plugins.HdfcGateway");
if (serviceType != null)
{
    var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(serviceType) as IPaymentGateway;
    // Register in DI dynamically
}

public class BankTransaction
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public decimal Amount { get; set; }
    public string UpiId { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

Reflection is used to build a generic audit log for payment requests: all properties marked [Sensitive] (AccountNumber, CVV, PIN) are masked automatically before writing to logs. This prevents accidental PCI-DSS violations without developers having to manually mask each field. Reflection reads the attributes once per type at startup and caches the PropertyInfo[] — so the per-request cost is just a dictionary lookup, not reflection on every request.

6. Interview Explanation

Always mention the performance cost (10-100x slower than direct call) and the caching solution (cache PropertyInfo[] in a static dictionary keyed by type). Mention Source Generators (Roslyn) as the modern alternative — they run at build time and generate type-safe code, eliminating runtime reflection overhead (this is how System.Text.Json source generation works).

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

ApproachWhenPerformance
ReflectionRuntimeSlow (cache results)
dynamicRuntime (DLR)Faster than reflection
Source GeneratorsBuild timeNative speed

8. Best Interview Answer

Reflection lets you inspect and interact with types at runtime — reading metadata, creating instances dynamically, invoking methods, and reading custom attributes. I have used it for: masking sensitive fields in payment request logs using a [Sensitive] attribute, building a plugin loader that discovers all IPaymentGateway implementations in dynamically loaded assemblies, and a generic CSV exporter that uses property names as column headers. The key concern is performance — reflection is 10-100x slower than direct calls. I always cache PropertyInfo arrays in a static dictionary keyed by type to pay the reflection cost once at startup, not per request.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Reflection = a mirror for your code. You can see everything about a type at runtime — its shape, properties, and methods — even things you didn't know existed when you wrote the code.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Reflection and dynamic in C#?
followUps.answer: dynamic uses the DLR (Dynamic Language Runtime) and is essentially syntactic sugar over reflection — it resolves method calls at runtime but with cleaner syntax. Reflection gives you full control: you can discover types, iterate members, read attributes. dynamic just lets you call methods without compile-time type checking. For inspecting metadata and attributes, you need reflection. For simply calling methods on an unknown type, dynamic is cleaner. Both are slow compared to static dispatch — neither belongs in tight loops.

questions.tip: Cache reflected metadata. Never call GetProperties() or GetMethod() inside a loop or per-request. Call it once at startup, store in a ConcurrentDictionary<Type, PropertyInfo[]>, reuse forever.

questions.question: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding in C#?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Method overloading means multiple methods in the same class share the same name but have different parameter lists — resolved at compile time. Method overriding means a derived class provides a new implementation of a virtual method from the base class with the same name and signature — resolved at runtime via the vtable.

2. Why It Is Used

Overloading provides convenience — multiple ways to call the same operation with different input forms. Overriding enables polymorphism — the correct behavior for the actual object type executes regardless of the reference type.

3. Advantages

  • Overloading: clean API — callers choose the most natural input form
  • Overriding: runtime polymorphism — add new types without changing existing code (OCP)
  • Together they cover both compile-time and runtime flexibility

4. Small C# Example

// METHOD OVERLOADING: same class, different parameters, compile-time resolved
public class PaymentService
{
    // Overload 1: amount only, uses default currency INR
    public PaymentResult Pay(decimal amount)
        => Pay(amount, "INR", null);

    // Overload 2: amount + currency
    public PaymentResult Pay(decimal amount, string currency)
        => Pay(amount, currency, null);

    // Overload 3: all parameters (primary implementation)
    public PaymentResult Pay(decimal amount, string currency, string? note)
    {
        Console.WriteLine($"Paying {amount} {currency}");
        return new PaymentResult(true);
    }

    // Return type alone doesn't create a valid overload:
    // public int Pay(decimal amount) -- COMPILE ERROR: ambiguous
}

// METHOD OVERRIDING: parent-child, same signature, runtime resolved
public abstract class FeeCalculator
{
    public abstract decimal Calculate(decimal amount);

    public string Describe(decimal amount)
        => $"Fee: {Calculate(amount):C}"; // calls overridden Calculate at runtime
}

public class UpiCalculator : FeeCalculator
{
    public override decimal Calculate(decimal amount)
        => amount <= 1000m ? 0m : amount * 0.005m; // UPI: free under 1000
}

public class NeftCalculator : FeeCalculator
{
    public override decimal Calculate(decimal amount)
        => amount <= 10000m ? 2.5m : 5m; // NEFT: flat fee
}

// Runtime polymorphism
FeeCalculator calc = GetCalculator("UPI"); // returns UpiCalculator
Console.WriteLine(calc.Describe(5000m));  // calls UpiCalculator.Calculate -- runtime dispatch

public record PaymentResult(bool Success);

static FeeCalculator GetCalculator(string type)
    => type == "UPI" ? new UpiCalculator() : new NeftCalculator();

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

PaymentService.Pay() is overloaded — the most convenient form for most callers is Pay(amount), while international payments use Pay(amount, currency). The fee calculator hierarchy uses overriding: UpiCalculator, NeftCalculator, and ImpsCalculator each override Calculate() with their own rate logic. The billing engine calls Describe() on any FeeCalculator reference — the correct Calculate() runs via virtual dispatch. Adding RTGS fees requires only a new RtgsCalculator class — no changes to the billing engine.

6. Interview Explanation

Interviewers often ask: "what does Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak() print?" — that tests runtime overriding. For overloading they ask: "can you overload by return type only?" — No, return type is not part of the overload signature. Only parameter types, count, and order count. Mention the key difference: overloading is compile-time; overriding is runtime.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FeatureOverloadingOverriding
ClassSame classParent-child
SignatureDifferent parametersSame signature
ResolutionCompile timeRuntime (vtable)
KeywordsNone specialvirtual + override

8. Best Interview Answer

Overloading is multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists in the same class — the compiler selects the correct one at compile time based on the arguments. Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads. Overriding is a derived class replacing a base class virtual method with the same signature — the CLR selects the correct implementation at runtime via vtable dispatch. Overloading is compile-time polymorphism; overriding is runtime polymorphism. In a fee calculator, overriding enables adding new payment types without changing existing code — the billing engine calls Calculate() and the vtable dispatches to the correct fee logic.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Overloading = same chef, different recipes for different orders (decided when you order = compile time). Overriding = new chef replaces the original but keeps the same menu item name (decided at serving time = runtime).

followUps.question: What is the classic polymorphism interview question: A a = new B(); which method runs?
followUps.answer:
public class A
{
    public virtual void Coding() => Console.WriteLine("A.Coding");
}

public class B : A
{
    public override void Coding() => Console.WriteLine("B.Coding");
}

// Question: which Coding() runs for each?
B b1 = new B();
b1.Coding();       // "B.Coding" -- obvious

A a  = new B();    // reference type = A, object type = B
a.Coding();        // "B.Coding" -- virtual dispatch, actual type wins!

// B b2 = new A();  -- COMPILE ERROR -- can't assign parent to child reference

The key insight: with virtual/override, the runtime type of the object determines which method runs, not the reference variable's declared type. A a = new B() — the variable is type A but the object is type B, so B.Coding() runs. This is runtime polymorphism. If the method used new instead of override, A.Coding() would run (method hiding, not overriding).

questions.tip: Memory aid: Overloading = 'many methods with same name in same class' (compile time). Overriding = 'child replaces parent's behaviour' (runtime). The 'virtual' keyword on base and 'override' on derived is what enables runtime polymorphism.

questions.question: What are the most commonly used String methods in C#? Give practical examples.
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

C# string provides a rich set of built-in methods for searching, extracting, modifying, splitting, and comparing text. String is immutable — every method returns a new string without modifying the original.

2. Why It Is Used

String manipulation is needed for input validation, data formatting, parsing API responses, building query parameters, and generating reports. Knowing the right method avoids unnecessary allocations and logic errors.

3. Advantages

  • Built-in methods cover all common string operations
  • Immutability makes strings safe to share across threads
  • StringComparison overloads prevent culture-related bugs
  • string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace is the correct validator for user input

4. Small C# Example

// Most commonly used string methods in a payment context
string upiId = "  Darshan@okICICI  ";

// Trim
string clean = upiId.Trim();                    // "Darshan@okICICI"

// Case
string lower = clean.ToLower();                 // "darshan@okicici"
string upper = clean.ToUpper();                 // "DARSHAN@OKICICI"

// Check
bool hasAt = clean.Contains('@');               // true
bool isUpi = clean.EndsWith("icici", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase); // true
bool starts = clean.StartsWith("Darshan");      // true

// Search
int atIndex = clean.IndexOf('@');               // 7
int lastAt = clean.LastIndexOf('@');            // 7

// Extract
string vpa = clean.Substring(0, atIndex);       // "Darshan"
string bank = clean[(atIndex + 1)..];           // "okICICI" (C# range syntax)

// Replace and Remove
string masked = clean.Replace(clean[..atIndex], "***"); // "***@okICICI"

// Split and Join
string csv = "HDFC,ICICI,SBI,AXIS";
string[] banks = csv.Split(',');                // ["HDFC","ICICI","SBI","AXIS"]
string joined = string.Join(" | ", banks);      // "HDFC | ICICI | SBI | AXIS"

// Null/Empty checks — always prefer IsNullOrWhiteSpace for user input
bool empty1 = string.IsNullOrEmpty(null);       // true
bool empty2 = string.IsNullOrEmpty("");          // true
bool empty3 = string.IsNullOrEmpty("  ");        // FALSE — whitespace not caught!
bool blank  = string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace("  ");   // true — correct for user input

// Case-insensitive comparison — always specify StringComparison
bool equal = string.Equals("upi", "UPI", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase); // true
// WRONG: "upi" == "UPI" -- false (case sensitive)

// Formatting
string accountDisplay = "ACC12345".PadLeft(12, '0'); // "0000ACC12345"
string amount = 5000m.ToString("N2");               // "5,000.00"

// String interpolation (C# 6+)
string receipt = $"Payment of {5000m:C} to {vpa} on {DateTime.UtcNow:dd-MMM-yyyy}";

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

UPI ID validation: Trim() removes accidental spaces from user input, ToLower() normalizes case, Contains('@') checks format, Split('@') extracts VPA and bank code. Account number masking for logs: the last 4 digits are shown, the rest replaced with *. Amount formatting: decimal.ToString("N2") for display, decimal.ToString("F4") for precision in database writes. PadLeft is used to format transaction IDs to fixed-width strings in fixed-format bank files.

6. Interview Explanation

The key interview trap: string.IsNullOrEmpty does not catch whitespace-only strings. Always use IsNullOrWhiteSpace for user input validation. Another trap: == for string comparison is culture-sensitive in some contexts — always specify StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase for case-insensitive comparisons to avoid culture-related bugs.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

MethodPurpose
IsNullOrEmptyChecks null or zero-length
IsNullOrWhiteSpaceChecks null, empty, or whitespace-only
Equals(OrdinalIgnoreCase)Case-insensitive, culture-safe comparison

8. Best Interview Answer

The most commonly used string methods: Trim/TrimStart/TrimEnd for whitespace, ToLower/ToUpper for normalization, Contains/StartsWith/EndsWith for searching, IndexOf/LastIndexOf for position, Substring and range syntax for extraction, Replace for modification, Split and Join for parsing and building, and IsNullOrWhiteSpace (not IsNullOrEmpty) for user input validation. Key rule: always use StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase for case-insensitive comparisons. Key trap: IsNullOrEmpty passes for whitespace-only strings — use IsNullOrWhiteSpace for user-provided data.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

String methods don't change the original — they return a new string. Think of every string as a receipt printout: you can make photocopies with modifications, but the original receipt never changes.

followUps.question: Why is string immutable in C# and what are the performance implications?
followUps.answer:
Strings in C# are immutable — every 'modification' creates a new string object. s.ToUpper() doesn't change s, it returns a new string. This has three implications:

  • Safety — strings can be safely shared between threads and cached without locking. A string passed to a method can't be modified by that method.
  • String interning — the CLR caches string literals in a pool. Two string literals with the same value are the same object in memory.
  • Performance — string concatenation in a loop creates N intermediate string objects. For 1000 concatenations you create 999 throwaway objects. Fix: use StringBuilder, string.Join(), or string.Concat() for multi-string operations.

Modern improvement: Span<char> and StringComparison let you operate on substrings without allocating new strings at all — useful in hot parsing paths.

questions.tip: Interview trap: Always use string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace() for validating user input — not IsNullOrEmpty(). A string of spaces passes IsNullOrEmpty but IsNullOrWhiteSpace catches it. Real user input often has accidental spaces.

questions.question: What are the types of inheritance in C#? Give examples.
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Inheritance in C# allows a class to acquire members from another class. There are five conceptual types: single (one parent), multilevel (chain: A→B→C), hierarchical (multiple children from one parent), multiple (via interfaces — classes cannot have multiple base classes), and hybrid (combination). C# enforces single class inheritance only.

2. Why It Is Used

Different types of inheritance model different real-world relationships. Knowing all types shows depth in OOP design — and knowing why C# chose single class inheritance (avoiding the diamond problem) shows architectural understanding.

3. Advantages

  • Single inheritance: simple, no ambiguity
  • Multilevel: model natural hierarchies (Account → SavingsAccount → PremiumSavings)
  • Hierarchical: shared base behavior across multiple types
  • Multiple via interfaces: flexibility without the diamond problem

4. Small C# Example

// 1. Single Inheritance
public class BankAccount { public string AccountNumber { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
public class SavingsAccount : BankAccount { public decimal InterestRate { get; } = 0.04m; }

// 2. Multilevel Inheritance: A -> B -> C
public class Account { public decimal Balance { get; protected set; } }
public class BankAcc : Account { public string Bank { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
public class PremiumAcc : BankAcc { public bool HasLocker { get; set; } }
// PremiumAcc has: Balance (from Account), Bank (from BankAcc), HasLocker (own)

// 3. Hierarchical Inheritance: multiple children, one parent
public class Payment { public decimal Amount { get; set; } }
public class UpiPayment : Payment { public string UpiId { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
public class CardPayment : Payment { public string CardLast4 { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
public class NeftPayment : Payment { public string Ifsc { get; set; } = string.Empty; }

// 4. Multiple Inheritance: C# uses interfaces (no multi-class inheritance)
public interface IRefundable  { Task RefundAsync(string txnId); }
public interface IDisputable  { Task RaiseDisputeAsync(string txnId, string reason); }

public class CreditCardPayment : Payment, IRefundable, IDisputable
{
    public async Task RefundAsync(string txnId) => Console.WriteLine($"Refund {txnId}");
    public async Task RaiseDisputeAsync(string txnId, string reason)
        => Console.WriteLine($"Dispute {txnId}: {reason}");
}

// 5. Hybrid: multilevel + hierarchical + interfaces
public class DigitalPayment : Payment, IRefundable
{
    public string TransactionRef { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public Task RefundAsync(string txnId) => Task.CompletedTask;
}
public class WalletPayment : DigitalPayment { public string WalletId { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
public class UpiWalletPayment : WalletPayment { } // multilevel chain continues

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

Payment is the base. Hierarchical: UpiPayment, CardPayment, NeftPayment all extend Payment. Multilevel: DigitalPayment extends Payment, WalletPayment extends DigitalPayment, UpiWalletPayment extends WalletPayment. Multiple (via interfaces): CreditCardPayment implements both IRefundable and IDisputable — it can do both operations. Hybrid: the entire payment hierarchy combines all three types in a real domain model.

6. Interview Explanation

Always mention WHY C# uses single class inheritance: the diamond problem. If class C inherits from both A and B, and both have a method X(), which X() does C get? C# avoids this by disallowing multiple base classes. Interfaces solve the multiple-type problem without ambiguity because interfaces don't provide implementation (pre-C#8), so there is no conflicting implementation to inherit.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

TypeStructureC# Support
SingleA → BYes
MultilevelA → B → CYes
HierarchicalA → B, A → CYes
Multiple (class)B, C → DNo (interfaces only)
HybridCombinationYes (with interfaces)

8. Best Interview Answer

C# supports five conceptual types of inheritance. Single: one class extends one base. Multilevel: a chain where B extends A and C extends B. Hierarchical: multiple classes extend the same base (UpiPayment, CardPayment both extend Payment). Multiple class inheritance is not allowed — C# avoids the diamond problem. Multiple type inheritance via interfaces is allowed — a class can implement many interfaces. Hybrid combines the above types. The design choice of single class inheritance makes the type system unambiguous while interfaces provide the flexibility of multiple contracts.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Single = one parent. Multilevel = grandparent chain. Hierarchical = multiple children, one parent. Multiple = C# says "only via interfaces" (no diamond drama). Hybrid = mix of all the above.

followUps.question: Why doesn't C# support multiple class inheritance?
What is the diamond problem?
Can a sealed class be inherited?

questions.tip: Always tie your answer to WHY C# made these design choices, not just list them.

questions.question: What are the types of errors in C#? How does exception handling work?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

C# errors fall into three categories: compile-time errors (syntax/type errors caught by the compiler), runtime errors (exceptions thrown during execution), and logical errors (code runs but produces wrong results). Exception handling uses try-catch-finally to detect and respond to runtime errors gracefully.

2. Why It Is Used

Without exception handling, any runtime error crashes the application. Try-catch-finally separates the normal execution path from the error-handling path, enables recovery or graceful degradation, ensures cleanup via finally, and allows specific handling for specific error types.

3. Advantages

  • Separates business logic from error handling
  • finally guarantees cleanup (close connections, release resources)
  • Specific catch blocks handle different errors differently
  • Custom exceptions carry domain context to callers

4. Small C# Example

// Three types of errors
// 1. Compile-time: caught by Roslyn, never reach runtime
// int x = "hello"; // ERROR: cannot convert string to int

// 2. Runtime error — exception
public async Task<WalletBalance> GetBalanceAsync(string accountId)
{
    try
    {
        // Could throw: NullReferenceException, SqlException, InvalidOperationException
        var account = await _repo.FindAsync(accountId);

        if (account == null)
            throw new AccountNotFoundException(accountId); // custom exception

        return new WalletBalance(account.Balance, account.Currency);
    }
    catch (AccountNotFoundException)
    {
        throw; // re-throw as-is, controller maps to 404
    }
    catch (SqlException ex) when (ex.Number == -2) // timeout
    {
        _logger.LogWarning("DB timeout for account {Id}", accountId);
        throw new ServiceUnavailableException("Database unavailable, retry later", ex);
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Unexpected error getting balance for {Id}", accountId);
        throw; // preserve stack trace
    }
    finally
    {
        // Always runs: log the operation, record metrics
        _metrics.RecordBalanceQuery(accountId);
    }
}

// Custom exception with domain context
public class AccountNotFoundException : Exception
{
    public string AccountId { get; }
    public AccountNotFoundException(string accountId)
        : base($"Account {accountId} not found")
    {
        AccountId = accountId;
    }
}

public record WalletBalance(decimal Balance, string Currency);

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

In a wallet balance API: AccountNotFoundException is a known business case — caught and re-thrown so the controller returns HTTP 404. SqlException timeout is a transient infrastructure error — logged and wrapped in ServiceUnavailableException for the caller to retry. Any other exception is unexpected — logged with full context and re-thrown. The finally block records a metrics event regardless of outcome for SLA monitoring.

6. Interview Explanation

Always mention three points: use throw (not throw ex) to preserve stack trace; catch most specific exceptions first; finally runs regardless of success or failure. Mention exception filters (when clause) as an advanced technique for conditional handling without unwinding the stack.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

Error TypeWhen DetectedHow Fixed
Compile-timeBuildFix syntax/types
RuntimeExecutiontry-catch-finally
LogicalTestingUnit tests, code review

8. Best Interview Answer

C# has three types of errors. Compile-time errors are caught by the compiler — wrong types, missing semicolons, undefined variables. Runtime errors are exceptions thrown during execution — null reference, divide by zero, SQL errors. Logical errors are code that runs but produces wrong output — only testing finds these. Exception handling uses try-catch-finally: try contains the risky code, catch handles specific exception types in order from most specific to most general, finally runs cleanup code always. Key rules: use throw not throw ex to preserve stack trace, catch exceptions in order from specific to general, never swallow exceptions silently.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

try = attempt the risky operation. catch = here is what to do if it fails. finally = always do this cleanup whether it succeeded or failed. Like: try opening a safe, catch if the code is wrong (log it), finally close the safe regardless.

followUps.question: Difference between throw and throw ex?
What is the finally block used for?
What are custom exceptions and when to create them?

questions.tip: Use 'throw;' not 'throw ex;' — the latter resets the stack trace.

questions.question: Why would you use multiple catch blocks? When is it useful?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Multiple catch blocks let you handle different exception types in different ways — each catch block is for a specific exception type, processed in order from most specific to most general. Catching exceptions generically in one block loses the ability to respond appropriately to each failure type.

2. Why It Is Used

Different exceptions require different responses: a database timeout should trigger a retry; a validation error should return a 400 to the client; a null reference is a bug that should be logged and rethrown. Treating all exceptions the same way makes error handling brittle.

3. Advantages

  • Appropriate response per exception type — retry, fallback, or rethrow
  • Exception filters (when) allow conditional catching without stack unwind
  • Specific error messages help callers understand what went wrong
  • Most-specific-first ordering is compiler-enforced

4. Small C# Example

public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessUpiAsync(UpiRequest request)
{
    try
    {
        ValidateRequest(request);
        var result = await _gateway.ChargeAsync(request.Amount, request.UpiId);
        return result;
    }
    catch (ArgumentException ex)
    {
        // Validation error: caller's fault — return 400, don't log as error
        _logger.LogWarning("Invalid UPI request: {Msg}", ex.Message);
        return PaymentResult.Failure($"Invalid input: {ex.Message}");
    }
    catch (GatewayTimeoutException ex)
    {
        // Transient: log and tell caller to retry
        _logger.LogWarning(ex, "Gateway timeout on UPI {UpiId}", request.UpiId);
        return PaymentResult.Transient("Gateway timeout — please retry");
    }
    catch (InsufficientFundsException ex)
    {
        // Known business case: no retry, surface to user
        _logger.LogInformation("Insufficient funds: {UpiId}", request.UpiId);
        return PaymentResult.Failure("Insufficient balance");
    }
    catch (DuplicateTransactionException ex)
    {
        // Idempotency: return the existing transaction result
        _logger.LogInformation("Duplicate txn detected: {Ref}", ex.ExistingRef);
        return await GetExistingTransactionResult(ex.ExistingRef);
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        // Unexpected: log everything and bubble up as 500
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Unexpected error processing UPI {UpiId}", request.UpiId);
        throw; // preserve stack trace
    }
}

// Exception filter: conditional catch without stack unwind
catch (SqlException ex) when (ex.Number == 1205) // deadlock only
{
    await RetryAfterDeadlockAsync();
}

public record UpiRequest(decimal Amount, string UpiId);

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A UPI payment can fail in five different ways that each need different handling: ArgumentException → return validation error to client. GatewayTimeoutException → retry up to 3 times. InsufficientFundsException → tell user to add money. DuplicateTransactionException → return the existing successful result (idempotency). Any other exception → log as critical and return 500. Without multiple catch blocks, all five scenarios get the same generic error message, making the payment system appear unreliable to users and developers alike.

6. Interview Explanation

Mention the ordering rule: most specific exception types must come before more general ones — the compiler enforces this (you cannot catch Exception before a more specific type in the same try block). Mention exception filters (when clause) as a way to narrow a catch without actually catching if the condition is false — the stack trace is preserved for diagnostics tools even when the condition is false.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

ApproachTrade-off
Single catch (Exception)Simple but loses response specificity
Multiple specific catchesVerbose but handles each case correctly
Exception filter (when)Conditional — best of both

8. Best Interview Answer

Multiple catch blocks let each exception type be handled appropriately. In a payment service: ArgumentException means invalid input — return 400, no retry. GatewayTimeoutException means transient failure — retry. InsufficientFundsException is a business condition — return a specific user message. Unexpected exceptions bubble up as 500 with full logging. Specific exceptions come before general ones — the compiler enforces ordering. Exception filters (when clause) allow further narrowing: catch SqlException only when the error number is a deadlock. The when expression is evaluated without unwinding the stack, preserving the full call stack for diagnostics.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Multiple catch blocks = different emergency procedures for different alarms. Fire alarm = evacuate. Medical alarm = call ambulance. Security alarm = call police. You wouldn't respond the same way to all alarms.

followUps.question: What is the 'when' keyword in catch blocks?
