questions.tip: Exception filters (when clause) are powerful — they don't unwind the stack if the condition is false.

questions.question: What are the types of collections in C#? Generic vs non-generic.
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

C# collections split into two generations: non-generic (System.Collections — legacy, store object, require casting) and generic (System.Collections.Generic — type-safe, no boxing). Generic collections are used exclusively in modern code.

2. Why It Is Used

Different collections are optimized for different operations. List<T> for indexed access, Dictionary<K,V> for O(1) lookup, HashSet<T> for unique membership tests, Queue<T> for FIFO, Stack<T> for LIFO. Choosing the wrong collection causes performance problems or incorrect behavior.

3. Advantages

  • Generic collections: type-safe, no boxing, IntelliSense support
  • Specialized collections (Queue, Stack, HashSet) model domain patterns precisely
  • Concurrent collections handle multi-threaded access safely
  • Immutable collections (ImmutableList, ImmutableDictionary) for thread-safe sharing

4. Small C# Example

// List<T>: ordered, dynamic, indexed access, LINQ-friendly
var transactions = new List<Transaction>();
transactions.Add(new Transaction(1, 5000m));
var large = transactions.Where(t => t.Amount > 1000m).ToList();

// Dictionary<K,V>: O(1) lookup by key
var accountCache = new Dictionary<string, Account>();
accountCache["ACC001"] = new Account("ACC001", 50000m);
if (accountCache.TryGetValue("ACC001", out Account? acc))
    Console.WriteLine(acc.Balance);

// HashSet<T>: unique values, O(1) membership check
var processedTxnIds = new HashSet<string>();
if (!processedTxnIds.Add(txnId)) // Add returns false if already exists
    throw new DuplicateTransactionException(txnId);

// Queue<T>: FIFO — payment processing queue
var pendingPayments = new Queue<PaymentRequest>();
pendingPayments.Enqueue(new PaymentRequest(1, 5000m));
pendingPayments.Enqueue(new PaymentRequest(2, 3000m));
var next = pendingPayments.Dequeue(); // first in, first out

// Stack<T>: LIFO — undo/redo, expression evaluation
var undoStack = new Stack<PaymentAction>();
undoStack.Push(new PaymentAction("Debit", 1000m));
var last = undoStack.Pop(); // last in, first out

// ConcurrentDictionary: thread-safe for multi-threaded payment processing
var rateCache = new System.Collections.Concurrent.ConcurrentDictionary<string, decimal>();
rateCache.TryAdd("USD", 83.5m);
decimal usdRate = rateCache.GetOrAdd("USD", _ => FetchRateFromApi("USD"));

// SortedDictionary: automatically sorted by key
var sortedFees = new SortedDictionary<decimal, string>
{
    { 100m, "Free" }, { 1000m, "Rs.2.50" }, { 100000m, "Rs.5.00" }
};

// Non-generic (legacy — avoid)
var legacyList = new System.Collections.ArrayList(); // stores object, boxes value types

record Transaction(int Id, decimal Amount);
record Account(string Id, decimal Balance);
record PaymentRequest(int Id, decimal Amount);
record PaymentAction(string Type, decimal Amount);
class DuplicateTransactionException : Exception
{
    public DuplicateTransactionException(string id) : base($"Duplicate: {id}") { }
}
static decimal FetchRateFromApi(string currency) => 83.5m;

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

Payment platform collections in practice: Dictionary<string, Account> as an in-memory account cache for O(1) lookups during validation. HashSet<string> of processed transaction IDs for idempotency — O(1) duplicate check. Queue<PaymentRequest> for the payment processing pipeline — first submitted, first processed. ConcurrentDictionary<string, decimal> for exchange rate cache — accessed by multiple payment threads simultaneously. Stack<PaymentAction> in the reversal workflow — payments reversed in reverse order.

6. Interview Explanation

Know the interface hierarchy: IEnumerable<T> (iterate only) → ICollection<T> (add/remove/count) → IList<T> (indexed access). Method parameters should accept the most restrictive interface that meets their needs — take IEnumerable<T> if you only iterate, IList<T> if you need indexes. This makes methods more flexible and testable.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

CollectionAddLookupUse Case
List<T>O(1)O(n) by value, O(1) by indexGeneral ordered list
Dictionary<K,V>O(1)O(1) by keyFast keyed lookup
HashSet<T>O(1)O(1) ContainsUnique values, deduplication
Queue<T>O(1)O(1) DequeueFIFO processing
Stack<T>O(1)O(1) PopLIFO (undo, parsing)

8. Best Interview Answer

C# has two generations of collections. Non-generic (ArrayList, Hashtable) are legacy — they store object, require casting, and box value types. Generic collections (System.Collections.Generic) are type-safe and should be used exclusively. List<T> is the default dynamic collection. Dictionary<K,V> for O(1) keyed lookups. HashSet<T> for unique values and fast membership tests. Queue<T> for FIFO. Stack<T> for LIFO. ConcurrentDictionary for thread-safe multi-threaded scenarios. The interface hierarchy IEnumerable → ICollection → IList matters for method signatures — accept the most restrictive interface that satisfies the method's needs.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

List = a shopping list (ordered, indexed). Dictionary = a phone book (look up by name instantly). HashSet = a bouncer's list (you're either on it or not, checked instantly). Queue = a checkout line (first in, first served). Stack = a stack of plates (last placed, first taken).

questions.tip: Know IEnumerable vs ICollection vs IList hierarchy — very common follow-up.

questions.question: What is the difference between Dispose() and Finalize() in C#?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Dispose() is a deterministic cleanup method you call explicitly (or via using) to release resources immediately. Finalize() (the ~ClassName() destructor) is called by the GC at an unpredictable future time as a last-resort safety net. Dispose() is always preferred — Finalize() is a backstop for when Dispose() was forgotten.

2. Why It Is Used

The GC only manages managed memory. Database connections, file handles, and network sockets are unmanaged resources that must be released promptly. Waiting for the GC's finalizer to clean them up causes connection pool exhaustion and file lock issues. Dispose() solves this with deterministic, immediate cleanup.

3. Advantages

  • Dispose() releases resources immediately — no waiting for GC
  • GC.SuppressFinalize() in Dispose() removes finalizer overhead
  • using statement guarantees Dispose() even on exceptions
  • IAsyncDisposable allows async cleanup (await using)

4. Small C# Example

public class PaymentDbContext : IDisposable
{
    private SqlConnection? _connection;
    private bool _disposed;

    public PaymentDbContext(string connectionString)
    {
        _connection = new SqlConnection(connectionString);
        _connection.Open();
    }

    // Public Dispose() — called by developer or using statement
    public void Dispose()
    {
        Dispose(disposing: true);
        GC.SuppressFinalize(this); // tell GC: skip the finalizer, already cleaned
    }

    // Protected virtual: derived classes can override to add their own cleanup
    protected virtual void Dispose(bool disposing)
    {
        if (_disposed) return;
        if (disposing)
        {
            // Managed resources: dispose them
            _connection?.Dispose();
            _connection = null;
        }
        // Unmanaged resources (if any): release here regardless of 'disposing'
        _disposed = true;
    }

    // Finalizer: safety net — called by GC if Dispose() was never called
    ~PaymentDbContext()
    {
        Dispose(disposing: false); // don't touch managed objects (may already be GC'd)
    }

    public async Task<decimal> GetBalanceAsync(string accountId)
    {
        ObjectDisposedException.ThrowIf(_disposed, this);
        // ... query
        return 50000m;
    }
}

// Always use 'using' — guarantees Dispose() on scope exit
using var ctx = new PaymentDbContext(connectionString);
var balance = await ctx.GetBalanceAsync("ACC001");
// ctx.Dispose() called here even if exception thrown

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A payment database context wraps a SqlConnection. If Dispose() is not called, the connection is returned to the pool only when the GC eventually calls the finalizer — possibly minutes later. In a high-traffic payment API with 1000 req/s, this exhausts the connection pool within seconds, causing all subsequent requests to fail with connection timeout errors. With using, each request disposes the context immediately on scope exit, returning the connection to the pool for the next request.

6. Interview Explanation

Key points: Dispose() = deterministic (you control when). Finalize() = non-deterministic (GC decides when). GC.SuppressFinalize(this) in Dispose() is a performance optimization — it removes the object from the finalization queue so it can be collected in the next GC cycle rather than surviving an extra cycle for finalization. Objects with finalizers survive one extra GC cycle — this is a performance cost, which is why GC.SuppressFinalize is important.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FeatureDispose()Finalize()
Who calls itDeveloper / usingGC
TimingImmediateNon-deterministic
ThreadCalling threadGC finalizer thread
RolePrimary cleanupSafety net only

8. Best Interview Answer

Dispose() is deterministic — you call it explicitly or via using and resources are released immediately. Finalize() is the GC's safety net — called at an unpredictable future time if Dispose() was never called. The standard pattern: implement Dispose(bool disposing) for the actual cleanup, public Dispose() calls it with true and calls GC.SuppressFinalize(this) to skip the finalizer since cleanup is already done. The finalizer calls Dispose(false) as a backstop. Objects with a finalizer survive an extra GC cycle — GC.SuppressFinalize avoids this overhead when Dispose() was called. Always prefer Dispose() via using over relying on the finalizer.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Dispose() = you clean your own room when you're done. Finalize() = your mum eventually cleans it if you forget. GC.SuppressFinalize = telling mum "I already cleaned it, don't bother."

followUps.question: When do you actually need a finalizer vs just IDisposable?

questions.tip: Key interview point: Dispose() is deterministic and explicit; Finalize() is non-deterministic and GC-driven. Always call GC.SuppressFinalize(this) inside Dispose() to avoid double-cleanup.

questions.question: What is yield return in C#? When do you use it?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

yield return is a keyword that transforms a method into a lazy iterator — the method produces values one at a time on demand instead of building a complete collection first. The compiler generates a state machine class that pauses and resumes execution at each yield return point.

2. Why It Is Used

For large data sets or streams, producing all items upfront wastes memory. yield return streams items to the caller one at a time — the caller can stop iterating early (with break) and none of the remaining items are computed. This is deferred execution.

3. Advantages

  • Zero memory for the full collection — items computed on demand
  • Early exit: caller can break the loop without computing remaining items
  • Foundation for LINQ's deferred execution model
  • Infinite sequences are possible (the method never runs out of items)

4. Small C# Example

// Without yield: builds entire list in memory
public List<Transaction> GetLargeTransactions(decimal threshold, int count)
{
    return _db.Transactions
        .Where(t => t.Amount > threshold)
        .Take(count)
        .ToList(); // all count items loaded into memory at once
}

// With yield: lazy — produces one item at a time
public IEnumerable<Transaction> StreamLargeTransactions(decimal threshold)
{
    foreach (var txn in _db.Transactions) // imagine reading from a file or stream
    {
        if (txn.Amount > threshold)
            yield return txn; // pause here, resume on next MoveNext()
    }
    // yield break: early exit from the iterator
}

// Caller can stop early — no wasted computation
foreach (var txn in StreamLargeTransactions(50000m))
{
    ProcessTransaction(txn);
    if (ShouldStop()) break; // stops iteration — remaining items never computed
}

// Real-world: reading a large transaction file line by line
public IEnumerable<string> ReadTransactionLines(string filePath)
{
    if (!File.Exists(filePath)) yield break; // early exit
    foreach (var line in File.ReadLines(filePath)) // already lazy
        if (!string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(line))
            yield return line;
}

// Using LINQ on top of yield (still lazy)
var highValueLines = ReadTransactionLines("txns.csv")
    .Where(l => l.Contains("HIGH"))  // LINQ deferred
    .Take(100);                       // only reads until 100 found

foreach (var line in highValueLines) // execution starts here
    ProcessLine(line);

public void ProcessTransaction(Transaction t) { }
public bool ShouldStop() => false;
public void ProcessLine(string line) { }
public class Transaction { public decimal Amount { get; set; } }
private static class _db { public static IEnumerable<Transaction> Transactions => new List<Transaction>(); }

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

Processing a month-end statement file with 2 million transaction lines: loading all 2 million lines into a List first requires gigabytes of memory. With yield return, the file is streamed line by line — only one line is in memory at a time. If the processor finds a critical error on line 100, it can break out of the loop and the remaining 1,999,900 lines are never even read from disk. This is critical for payment batch processing systems that run on memory-constrained environments.

6. Interview Explanation

Mention the compiler-generated state machine — each yield return is a state in that machine. The method body pauses at yield return and resumes from exactly that point on the next MoveNext() call. Key constraint: you cannot yield return inside a catch block (though you can in try and finally). Mention that LINQ's deferred execution (Where, Select, etc.) is built on the same mechanism.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

ApproachMemoryEarly ExitBest For
Return List<T>All items at onceN/A (already built)Small collections
yield returnOne item at a timeYes — break worksLarge streams, files

8. Best Interview Answer

yield return turns a method into a lazy iterator — instead of building a full collection and returning it, the method produces one item at a time on demand. The compiler generates a state machine that pauses at each yield return and resumes on the next MoveNext() call. Benefits: zero memory for the full collection, early exit works (break stops the iteration immediately), and infinite sequences are possible. I use it for streaming large transaction files line by line, for generating report rows lazily from a database cursor, and for any scenario where the caller may not need all items. LINQ's Where and Select are built on the same deferred execution model.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

yield return = a vending machine that dispenses one snack when you press the button — it doesn't fill all slots upfront. You get one item, come back for the next, and can walk away anytime without wasting the rest.

followUps.question: What happens to the state machine the compiler generates for yield?

questions.tip: yield return = lazy iteration via compiler-generated state machine. Best for large data streams where you don't need all results at once. The method pauses and resumes on each MoveNext() call.

questions.question: What are anonymous types in C#? When would you use them?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Anonymous types are compiler-generated classes with read-only properties created inline using the new { } syntax without specifying a type name. They are immutable, scope-limited (cannot cross method boundaries as typed returns), and mainly used for LINQ projections.

2. Why It Is Used

In LINQ Select() projections inside a method, anonymous types let you shape query results into a lightweight structure without creating a named DTO class for every intermediate query shape. Modern C# records are a better alternative when the shape needs to cross method boundaries.

3. Advantages

  • No need to define a class for every intermediate query shape
  • Compiler generates Equals, GetHashCode, ToString automatically
  • Two anonymous types with the same property names, types, and order are the same compiler type
  • Properties are read-only — prevents accidental mutation

4. Small C# Example

// Anonymous type: properties read-only, type is compiler-generated
var summary = new { AccountId = "ACC001", Balance = 50000m, Currency = "INR" };
Console.WriteLine(summary.AccountId); // "ACC001"
// summary.Balance = 60000m; // COMPILE ERROR — read-only

// Most common use: LINQ projection inside a method
var transactionSummaries = transactions
    .Where(t => t.Date >= startDate)
    .GroupBy(t => t.AccountId)
    .Select(g => new
    {
        AccountId = g.Key,
        TotalDebits = g.Where(t => t.Type == "Debit").Sum(t => t.Amount),
        TotalCredits = g.Where(t => t.Type == "Credit").Sum(t => t.Amount),
        TransactionCount = g.Count()
    })
    .OrderByDescending(s => s.TotalDebits)
    .ToList();

foreach (var s in transactionSummaries)
    Console.WriteLine($"{s.AccountId}: Debits={s.TotalDebits:C}, Credits={s.TotalCredits:C}");

// Problem: cannot return anonymous type from public method
// public ??? GetSummary() => new { AccountId = "x", Balance = 100m }; // no return type!

// Solution 1: Use a named record (modern C# — preferred)
public record TransactionSummary(string AccountId, decimal TotalDebits, decimal TotalCredits, int Count);

// Solution 2: Use a DTO class
public class AccountSummaryDto
{
    public string AccountId { get; init; } = string.Empty;
    public decimal TotalDebits { get; init; }
    public decimal TotalCredits { get; init; }
}

// Anonymous type equality: same property names/types/order = same type
var a = new { X = 1, Y = 2 };
var b = new { X = 3, Y = 4 };
Console.WriteLine(a.GetType() == b.GetType()); // true — same compiler type

public record Transaction(string AccountId, DateTime Date, decimal Amount, string Type);
private DateTime startDate = DateTime.UtcNow.AddMonths(-1);
private List<Transaction> transactions = new();

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A payment dashboard query groups transactions by account, computes debits and credits, and projects to an anonymous type for display in a single method. The anonymous type is never returned to the caller — it stays within the method scope. For the API response that crosses service boundaries, a proper AccountSummaryDto record is used — typed, named, and documented. Anonymous types bridge the gap between the raw entity and the final DTO in the middle of a LINQ chain.

6. Interview Explanation

Key limitation to mention: anonymous types are scope-limited. They cannot be returned from public methods (no compile-time name), stored in class fields, or used as method parameters of a known type. For anything crossing a method boundary, use C# records (recommended for C# 9+) or DTOs. Anonymous types are a compile-time feature — they do not use reflection.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

FeatureAnonymous TypeRecord
NamedNoYes
Return from methodNo (only as object)Yes
ImmutableYesYes (init-only)
C# versionC# 3+C# 9+

8. Best Interview Answer

Anonymous types are compiler-generated immutable classes created with new { Property = value }. They are used primarily for LINQ Select() projections inside a method — shaping data without creating a named class for every intermediate query result. Properties are read-only, equality is value-based, and the compiler generates ToString automatically. The key limitation: anonymous types are scope-limited — they cannot be returned from public methods by type name. For data shapes that cross method boundaries, I use C# record types (C# 9+) — they give the same conciseness with named types that can be returned, passed, and documented.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Anonymous type = a sticky note (quick, temporary, local use only). Record = a printed form (named, permanent, shareable). Use sticky notes inside a method; use printed forms when the data needs to leave.

followUps.question: Why can't you return an anonymous type from a public method?
How do anonymous types differ from C# records?
When would you use dynamic vs anonymous type for holding ad-hoc data?

questions.tip: Anonymous types = read-only, compiler-generated, scope-limited. Great for LINQ projections. For anything leaving a method, use a named DTO or record instead.

questions.question: What causes memory leaks in C#? How do you detect and fix them?
questions.answer:
1. Simple Definition

Memory leaks in .NET occur when objects are kept alive by unintended root references longer than needed — the GC cannot collect them because it sees them as still in use. Common causes: unsubscribed event handlers, static collections that grow forever, undisposed IDisposable objects, and closures capturing large objects.

2. Why It Is Used

Understanding memory leaks is essential for building high-availability payment systems. A leak that adds 1MB per request creates an out-of-memory crash after enough requests. In fintech, memory crashes during peak transaction periods are critical failures.

3. Advantages

  • Knowing common leak patterns prevents them in design
  • dotnet-counters, dotnet-dump allow production diagnosis without restarts
  • WeakReference allows caching without preventing GC collection

4. Small C# Example

// LEAK 1: Event handler not unsubscribed
public class TransactionMonitor
{
    public event EventHandler<TransactionEventArgs>? TransactionReceived;
}

// BAD: subscriber leaks because TransactionMonitor holds a reference to Dashboard
public class Dashboard
{
    public Dashboard(TransactionMonitor monitor)
    {
        monitor.TransactionReceived += OnTransactionReceived; // LEAK!
        // monitor holds ref to Dashboard via event handler
        // Dashboard cannot be GC'd as long as monitor lives
    }

    private void OnTransactionReceived(object? s, TransactionEventArgs e) { }
}

// FIXED: implement IDisposable and unsubscribe
public class DashboardFixed : IDisposable
{
    private readonly TransactionMonitor _monitor;

    public DashboardFixed(TransactionMonitor monitor)
    {
        _monitor = monitor;
        _monitor.TransactionReceived += OnTransactionReceived;
    }

    public void Dispose()
        => _monitor.TransactionReceived -= OnTransactionReceived; // unsubscribe!

    private void OnTransactionReceived(object? s, TransactionEventArgs e) { }
}

// LEAK 2: Static collection that grows forever
public static class AuditLog
{
    // LEAK: never cleared, grows for lifetime of app
    private static readonly List<string> _entries = new();

    public static void Add(string entry) => _entries.Add(entry);
    // No size limit, no eviction — eventually OOM
}

// FIXED: use IMemoryCache with expiration
// _memoryCache.Set(key, value, TimeSpan.FromMinutes(30));

// LEAK 3: Not disposing IDisposable
void BadMethod()
{
    var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr); // LEAK: not disposed
    conn.Open();
    // exception here = connection never closed, pool exhausted
}

void GoodMethod()
{
    using var conn = new SqlConnection(connStr); // FIXED: always disposed
    conn.Open();
}

// LEAK 4: Closure capturing a large object
static Action? _action;
void RegisterAction()
{
    var largeBuffer = new byte[1_000_000]; // 1MB
    _action = () => Console.WriteLine(largeBuffer.Length); // closure captures buffer!
    // largeBuffer cannot be GC'd while _action is alive
}

public class TransactionEventArgs : EventArgs { }
private static string connStr = string.Empty;

5. Real-Time Fintech Example

A real-time payment dashboard subscribes to TransactionMonitor events for live updates. When the dashboard page is closed and the component is disposed, the event subscription is not removed — TransactionMonitor holds the Dashboard instance alive via the event delegate. Over time, thousands of closed but undisposed Dashboard instances accumulate in memory. At peak trading times, the server runs out of memory and crashes. Fix: Dashboard implements IDisposable and unsubscribes in Dispose(), and the DI container disposes it when the request scope ends.

6. Interview Explanation

The four leak patterns to always mention: event handlers (most common), static collections (second most common), undisposed IDisposable, and closures. For detection: dotnet-counters for live GC metrics, dotnet-dump for heap snapshots, Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools for local investigation. For prevention: IDisposable + using for all resource classes, WeakEvent patterns for long-lived publishers, IMemoryCache with expiration for shared caches.

7. Difference Table (if applicable)

Leak CauseDetectionFix
Event handlersHeap snapshot growthUnsubscribe in Dispose()
Static collectionsGen2 size growthIMemoryCache with expiration
Undisposed IDisposableConnection pool errorsusing statement
Closure capturesLarge objects in Gen2Capture only what you need

8. Best Interview Answer

Memory leaks in .NET happen when the GC cannot collect objects because live references still point to them. The four most common causes: event handlers not unsubscribed (publisher holds subscriber alive via the delegate), static collections that grow without bound (no eviction policy), IDisposable objects not disposed (database connections pool exhaustion), and closures capturing large objects that cannot be collected while the delegate is alive. Detection: dotnet-counters to watch Gen2 size trend, dotnet-dump to take a heap snapshot and find which types are growing, Visual Studio memory profiler for local investigation. Prevention: IDisposable + unsubscribe in Dispose() for event subscriptions, IMemoryCache with sliding expiration for shared caches, using statement for all IDisposable objects.

9. Easy Trick to Remember

Memory leak = leaving doors open that the GC cannot close because it thinks someone is still using them. The four open doors: events (close with -=), static caches (close with expiration), connections (close with using), closures (close by not capturing large objects).
